Caption: Differential voltage (DV) plot of a fresh LGM-50T commercial cell generated from constant current discharge data at a C / 20 current rate. The peaks associated with the negative electrode (anode) are marked with “n” and the peaks associated with the positive electrode (cathode) are marked with “p”. BOD and EOD stand for beginning of discharge and end of discharge, respectively.

The ratio of negative electrode capacity ( Q n ), positive electrode capacity ( Q p ), and full cell capacity ( Q F C ) between the final RPTx and the first RPTx performed at the beginning of life. The plot illustrates these ratios at different storage SOCs and at storage temperatures of (a) 0 °C, (b) 25 °C, and (c) 45 °C.

Schematic of the computational domain for the electrochemical and degradation model. The upper rectangle with 1D Cartesian coordinates x ∈ [ 0 , L ] represents the porous electrode region. Blue and red spheres with radial coordinates r ∈ [ 0 , R k ] ( k ∈ { n , p } ) denote representative particles in negative and positive electrodes, respectively. The dominant degradation mechanisms, which are the growth of the SEI layer and associated pore clogging in the negative electrode, are also illustrated. Grey patches in the negative electrode, indicate areas where particles become inaccessible for intercalation due to pore clogging. Governing equations describing these degradation mechanisms in physics-based and UDE models are presented below the schematic. See the main text for an explanation of the individual terms in the models.

Comparison of capacity predictions by the physics-based model and UDE model against experimental data for cells stored at 45 °C across different storage SOCs. The storage SOC value is given as title for each sub-figure.
